content:
type: SURVEY
files: outlines/prompts.py
note: Adding custom filters support: prompt decorator gets optional filters param (usable as @prompt or @prompt(filters={...})), Prompt.from_str/from_file gain filters param, new create_jinja_env() helper centralizes jinja2.Environment creation + filter registration, _template_from_str/_template_from_file refactored to use it. Touches: prompt() decorator, Prompt.from_str, Prompt.from_file, Prompt._template_from_str, Prompt._template_from_file, plus new create_jinja_env function.

fields.note: Adding custom filters support: prompt decorator gets optional filters param (usable as @prompt or @prompt(filters={...})), Prompt.from_str/from_file gain filters param, new create_jinja_env() helper centralizes jinja2.Environment creation + filter registration, _template_from_str/_template_from_file refactored to use it. Touches: prompt() decorator, Prompt.from_str, Prompt.from_file, Prompt._template_from_str, Prompt._template_from_file, plus new create_jinja_env function.

content:
type: DRAFT
files: outlines/prompts.py
region: module-level filter registry (register_filter/unregister_filter), create_jinja_env(), Prompt.from_str/from_file, Prompt._template_from_str/_template_from_file, prompt() decorator
code: _FILTERS: Dict[str, Callable] = {}


def register_filter(name: str, filter_fn: Callable) -> None:
    """Register a global Jinja2 filter available to all prompt templates."""
    _FILTERS[name] = filter_fn


def unregister_filter(name: str) -> None:
    """Remove a previously registered global Jinja2 filter."""
    _FILTERS.pop(name, None)


def create_jinja_env(
    loader: Optional[jinja2.BaseLoader] = None,
    filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None,
) -> jinja2.Environment:
    """Create a Jinja2 environment with the standard Outlines configuration.

    Registers the built-in filters, any globally registered filters, and
    any filters passed explicitly (which take precedence over global ones).

    """
    env = jinja2.Environment(
        loader=loader,
        trim_blocks=True,
        lstrip_blocks=True,
        keep_trailing_newline=True,
        undefined=jinja2.StrictUndefined,
    )
    env.filters["name"] = get_fn_name
    env.filters["description"] = get_fn_description
    env.filters["source"] = get_fn_source
    env.filters["signature"] = get_fn_signature
    env.filters["schema"] = get_schema
    env.filters["args"] = get_fn_args

    env.filters.update(_FILTERS)
    if filters:
        env.filters.update(filters)

    return env


@classmethod
def from_str(cls, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create an instance of the class from a string.

    Parameters
    ----------
    content : str
        The string content to be converted into a template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        A dictionary of custom Jinja2 filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    An instance of the class with the provided content as a template.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_str(content, filters), None)

@classmethod
def from_file(cls, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create a Prompt instance from a file containing a Jinja template.

    Note: This method does not allow to include and inheritance to reference files
    that are outside the folder or subfolders of the file given to `from_file`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    path : Path
        The path to the file containing the Jinja template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        A dictionary of custom Jinja2 filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    Prompt
        An instance of the Prompt class with the template loaded from the file.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_file(path, filters), None)

@classmethod
def _template_from_str(_, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    # Dedent, and remove extra linebreak
    cleaned_template = inspect.cleandoc(content)

    # Add linebreak if there were any extra linebreaks that
    # `cleandoc` would have removed
    ends_with_linebreak = content.replace(" ", "").endswith("\n\n")
    if ends_with_linebreak:
        cleaned_template += "\n"

    # Remove extra whitespaces, except those that immediately follow a newline symbol.
    # This is necessary to avoid introducing whitespaces after backslash `\` characters
    # used to continue to the next line without linebreak.
    cleaned_template = re.sub(r"(?![\r\n])(\b\s+)", " ", cleaned_template)

    env = create_jinja_env(None, filters)

    return env.from_string(cleaned_template)

@classmethod
def _template_from_file(_, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    file_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(path))
    env = create_jinja_env(jinja2.FileSystemLoader(file_directory), filters)
    return env.get_template(os.path.basename(path))


def prompt(
    fn: Optional[Callable] = None, *, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None
) -> Union[Prompt, Callable[[Callable], Prompt]]:
    """Decorate a function that contains a prompt template.

    This allows to define prompts in the docstring of a function and simplify their
    manipulation by providing some degree of encapsulation. It uses the `render`
    function internally to render templates.

    >>> import outlines
    >>>
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    >>> def build_prompt(question):
    ...    "I have a ${question}"
    ...
    >>> prompt = build_prompt("How are you?")

    This API can also be helpful in an "agent" context where parts of the prompt
    are set when the agent is initialized and never modified later. In this situation
    we can partially apply the prompt function at initialization.

    >>> import outlines
    >>> import functools as ft
    ...
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    ... def solve_task(name: str, objective: str, task: str):
    ...     '''Your name is {{name}}.
    ..      Your overall objective is to {{objective}}.
    ...     Please solve the following task: {{task}}
    ...     '''
    ...
    >>> hal = ft.partial(solve_task, "HAL", "Travel to Jupiter")

    Custom Jinja2 filters can be passed in as well:

    >>> @outlines.prompt(filters={"reverse": lambda s: s[::-1]})
    ... def reverse_prompt(text):
    ...     '''{{ text | reverse }}'''

    Parameters
    ----------
    fn
        The function whose docstring contains the template to render.
    filters
        A dictionary of custom Jinja2 filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    A `Prompt` callable class which will render the template when called.

    """

    def decorator(fn: Callable) -> Prompt:
        signature = inspect.signature(fn)

        # The docstring contains the template that will be rendered to be used
        # as a prompt to the language model.
        docstring = fn.__doc__
        if docstring is None:
            raise TypeError("Could not find a template in the function's docstring.")

        template = Prompt._template_from_str(cast(str, docstring), filters)

        return Prompt(template, signature)

    if fn is not None:
        return decorator(fn)

    return decorator
note: Merged both features: global registry (_FILTERS/register_filter/unregister_filter) + create_jinja_env(loader, filters) merges built-ins -> _FILTERS -> explicit filters (explicit wins). prompt() now supports bare @prompt and @prompt(filters={...}) via inner decorator + fn is None check. Also need 'from typing import ... Union' added to imports (Optional/Dict/Callable/cast already imported). Let me know if this matches your draft.

code: _FILTERS: Dict[str, Callable] = {}


def register_filter(name: str, func: Callable) -> None:
    """Register a custom filter globally so it is available to all prompt templates."""
    _FILTERS[name] = func


def unregister_filter(name: str) -> None:
    """Remove a previously registered global filter."""
    _FILTERS.pop(name, None)


def create_jinja_env(
    filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None,
    loader: Optional[jinja2.BaseLoader] = None,
) -> jinja2.Environment:
    """Create the Jinja2 environment used to render prompt templates.

    Parameters
    ----------
    filters
        Custom filters to make available in the template, in addition to the
        default Outlines filters and any filters registered globally via
        `register_filter`. Locally-provided filters take precedence over
        globally registered ones.
    loader
        An optional Jinja2 loader, used so templates loaded from a file can
        resolve `include`/`extends` statements relative to their directory.

    """
    env = jinja2.Environment(
        loader=loader,
        trim_blocks=True,
        lstrip_blocks=True,
        keep_trailing_newline=True,
        undefined=jinja2.StrictUndefined,
    )
    env.filters["name"] = get_fn_name
    env.filters["description"] = get_fn_description
    env.filters["source"] = get_fn_source
    env.filters["signature"] = get_fn_signature
    env.filters["schema"] = get_schema
    env.filters["args"] = get_fn_args
    env.filters.update(_FILTERS)
    if filters:
        env.filters.update(filters)

    return env


@classmethod
def from_str(cls, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create an instance of the class from a string.

    Parameters
    ----------
    content : str
        The string content to be converted into a template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        Custom filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    An instance of the class with the provided content as a template.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_str(content, filters), None)


@classmethod
def from_file(cls, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create a Prompt instance from a file containing a Jinja template.

    Note: This method does not allow to include and inheritance to reference files
    that are outside the folder or subfolders of the file given to `from_file`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    path : Path
        The path to the file containing the Jinja template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        Custom filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    Prompt
        An instance of the Prompt class with the template loaded from the file.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_file(path, filters), None)


@classmethod
def _template_from_str(_, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    # Dedent, and remove extra linebreak
    cleaned_template = inspect.cleandoc(content)

    # Add linebreak if there were any extra linebreaks that
    # `cleandoc` would have removed
    ends_with_linebreak = content.replace(" ", "").endswith("\n\n")
    if ends_with_linebreak:
        cleaned_template += "\n"

    # Remove extra whitespaces, except those that immediately follow a newline symbol.
    # This is necessary to avoid introducing whitespaces after backslash `\` characters
    # used to continue to the next line without linebreak.
    cleaned_template = re.sub(r"(?![\r\n])(\b\s+)", " ", cleaned_template)

    env = create_jinja_env(filters)

    return env.from_string(cleaned_template)


@classmethod
def _template_from_file(_, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    file_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(path))
    env = create_jinja_env(filters, loader=jinja2.FileSystemLoader(file_directory))
    return env.get_template(os.path.basename(path))


def prompt(
    fn: Optional[Callable] = None, *, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None
) -> Union[Prompt, Callable[[Callable], Prompt]]:
    """Decorate a function that contains a prompt template.

    This allows to define prompts in the docstring of a function and simplify their
    manipulation by providing some degree of encapsulation. It uses the `render`
    function internally to render templates.

    >>> import outlines
    >>>
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    >>> def build_prompt(question):
    ...    "I have a ${question}"
    ...
    >>> prompt = build_prompt("How are you?")

    This API can also be helpful in an "agent" context where parts of the prompt
    are set when the agent is initialized and never modified later. In this situation
    we can partially apply the prompt function at initialization.

    >>> import outlines
    >>> import functools as ft
    ...
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    ... def solve_task(name: str, objective: str, task: str):
    ...     '''Your name is {{name}}.
    ..      Your overall objective is to {{objective}}.
    ...     Please solve the following task: {{task}}
    ...     '''
    ...
    >>> hal = ft.partial(solve_task, "HAL", "Travel to Jupiter")

    Custom filters can be passed either locally to a single prompt, or
    registered globally with `register_filter` so they are available to
    every prompt template.

    >>> @outlines.prompt(filters={"shout": str.upper})
    ... def greet(name):
    ...     "{{ name | shout }}"

    Returns
    -------
    A `Prompt` callable class which will render the template when called.

    """

    def decorator(fn: Callable) -> Prompt:
        signature = inspect.signature(fn)

        # The docstring contains the template that will be rendered to be used
        # as a prompt to the language model.
        docstring = fn.__doc__
        if docstring is None:
            raise TypeError("Could not find a template in the function's docstring.")

        template = Prompt._template_from_str(cast(str, docstring), filters)

        return Prompt(template, signature)

    if fn is None:
        return decorator
    else:
        return decorator(fn)

def register_filter(name: str, func: Callable) -> None:
    """Register a custom filter globally so it is available to all prompt templates."""
    _FILTERS[name] = func


def unregister_filter(name: str) -> None:
    """Remove a previously registered global filter."""
    _FILTERS.pop(name, None)


def create_jinja_env(
    filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None,
    loader: Optional[jinja2.BaseLoader] = None,
) -> jinja2.Environment:
    """Create the Jinja2 environment used to render prompt templates.

    Parameters
    ----------
    filters
        Custom filters to make available in the template, in addition to the
        default Outlines filters and any filters registered globally via
        `register_filter`. Locally-provided filters take precedence over
        globally registered ones.
    loader
        An optional Jinja2 loader, used so templates loaded from a file can
        resolve `include`/`extends` statements relative to their directory.

    """
    env = jinja2.Environment(
        loader=loader,
        trim_blocks=True,
        lstrip_blocks=True,
        keep_trailing_newline=True,
        undefined=jinja2.StrictUndefined,
    )
    env.filters["name"] = get_fn_name
    env.filters["description"] = get_fn_description
    env.filters["source"] = get_fn_source
    env.filters["signature"] = get_fn_signature
    env.filters["schema"] = get_schema
    env.filters["args"] = get_fn_args
    env.filters.update(_FILTERS)
    if filters:
        env.filters.update(filters)

    return env


@classmethod
def from_str(cls, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create an instance of the class from a string.

    Parameters
    ----------
    content : str
        The string content to be converted into a template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        Custom filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    An instance of the class with the provided content as a template.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_str(content, filters), None)


@classmethod
def from_file(cls, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None):
    """
    Create a Prompt instance from a file containing a Jinja template.

    Note: This method does not allow to include and inheritance to reference files
    that are outside the folder or subfolders of the file given to `from_file`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    path : Path
        The path to the file containing the Jinja template.
    filters : Optional[Dict[str, Callable]]
        Custom filters to make available in the template.

    Returns
    -------
    Prompt
        An instance of the Prompt class with the template loaded from the file.
    """
    return cls(cls._template_from_file(path, filters), None)


@classmethod
def _template_from_str(_, content: str, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    # Dedent, and remove extra linebreak
    cleaned_template = inspect.cleandoc(content)

    # Add linebreak if there were any extra linebreaks that
    # `cleandoc` would have removed
    ends_with_linebreak = content.replace(" ", "").endswith("\n\n")
    if ends_with_linebreak:
        cleaned_template += "\n"

    # Remove extra whitespaces, except those that immediately follow a newline symbol.
    # This is necessary to avoid introducing whitespaces after backslash `\` characters
    # used to continue to the next line without linebreak.
    cleaned_template = re.sub(r"(?![\r\n])(\b\s+)", " ", cleaned_template)

    env = create_jinja_env(filters)

    return env.from_string(cleaned_template)


@classmethod
def _template_from_file(_, path: Path, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None) -> jinja2.Template:
    file_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(path))
    env = create_jinja_env(filters, loader=jinja2.FileSystemLoader(file_directory))
    return env.get_template(os.path.basename(path))


def prompt(
    fn: Optional[Callable] = None, *, filters: Optional[Dict[str, Callable]] = None
) -> Union[Prompt, Callable[[Callable], Prompt]]:
    """Decorate a function that contains a prompt template.

    This allows to define prompts in the docstring of a function and simplify their
    manipulation by providing some degree of encapsulation. It uses the `render`
    function internally to render templates.

    >>> import outlines
    >>>
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    >>> def build_prompt(question):
    ...    "I have a ${question}"
    ...
    >>> prompt = build_prompt("How are you?")

    This API can also be helpful in an "agent" context where parts of the prompt
    are set when the agent is initialized and never modified later. In this situation
    we can partially apply the prompt function at initialization.

    >>> import outlines
    >>> import functools as ft
    ...
    >>> @outlines.prompt
    ... def solve_task(name: str, objective: str, task: str):
    ...     '''Your name is {{name}}.
    ..      Your overall objective is to {{objective}}.
    ...     Please solve the following task: {{task}}
    ...     '''
    ...
    >>> hal = ft.partial(solve_task, "HAL", "Travel to Jupiter")

    Custom filters can be passed either locally to a single prompt, or
    registered globally with `register_filter` so they are available to
    every prompt template.

    >>> @outlines.prompt(filters={"shout": str.upper})
    ... def greet(name):
    ...     "{{ name | shout }}"

    Returns
    -------
    A `Prompt` callable class which will render the template when called.

    """

    def decorator(fn: Callable) -> Prompt:
        signature = inspect.signature(fn)

        # The docstring contains the template that will be rendered to be used
        # as a prompt to the language model.
        docstring = fn.__doc__
        if docstring is None:
            raise TypeError("Could not find a template in the function's docstring.")

        template = Prompt._template_from_str(cast(str, docstring), filters)

        return Prompt(template, signature)

    if fn is None:
        return decorator
    else:
        return decorator(fn)
